LES Ecology Center Street Tree Care Map

LES Ecology Center · East Village · Lower East Side · Chinatown

What is this?

This tool helps the LES Ecology Center's tree stewardship program prioritize which street trees need care. It covers the Community Board 3 area — the best geographic proxy for where LES Ecology Center works — mapping all 4,939 street trees in the neighborhood, overlaid with LES Ecology Center's ESCR planting records (2019–2024), and ranks every tree by care urgency using a simple two-factor system.

Who is it for?

LES Ecology Center street tree care staff — to plan watering and care walks, identify where new ESCR plantings are concentrated, and focus effort where it matters most.

How to use it

🗺
Map tab
View all street trees in the CB3 area. By default, dots are colored by priority tier (red = highest need, green = lowest). Toggle to Species View to see tree diversity. Filter by health or species using the dropdowns. Click 🌱 Show New Plantings to overlay LES Ecology Center ESCR plantings.
📊
Stats tab
Summary statistics for the current filter: health breakdown, average priority tier, and the top 10 most common species.
🏙
Top Blocks tab
The 100 street blocks with the most high-priority trees, ranked by urgency. Use this to plan stewardship walks. Click any dot on the map to see a full breakdown of that tree's priority score. Download the list as CSV for offline use.

Priority tiers explained

Each tree is assigned one of 6 tiers based on two yes/no questions:

Tier 6 — Young/ESCR-planted · on a commercial corridor
Tier 5 — Young/ESCR-planted · high foot-traffic area
Tier 4 — Young/ESCR-planted · residential street
Tier 3 — Mature tree · commercial corridor
Tier 2 — Mature tree · high foot-traffic area
Tier 1 — Mature tree · residential street

A tree is "young" if its trunk diameter is under 6 inches, or if it appears in LES Ecology Center's ESCR planting records. Young trees need the most attention in their first years to establish healthy root systems.

Data sources

NYC 2015 Street Tree Census — NYC Parks & Recreation / NYC Open Data. The most recent citywide street tree census, providing species, health, trunk diameter, and location for all 4,939 trees in the CB3 area.
LES Ecology Center ESCR Planting Records (2019–2024) — LES Ecology Center. Records of street trees planted through LES Ecology Center's Enhanced Street Tree programs, including species, address, and planting season.
NYC Planning Geosearch API — NYC Department of City Planning. Used to geocode ESCR planting addresses to map coordinates.

Note: Census data is from 2015. Tree conditions and counts may have changed. This map is a planning aid, not a definitive record.

Created by Hadas Fischer, Street Tree Care Manager, LES Ecology Center
March 2026

How This Works

Methodology for the Top 100 Blocks list

Priority Score Formula

Each street tree is assigned a priority score from 0–100, combining two factors:

70%
🌱 Young or newly planted tree
A tree qualifies if its trunk diameter (DBH) is under 6 inches, or if it appears in LES Ecology Center's ESCR planting records. Young trees need the most care in their first years to establish healthy root systems.
30%
👁 Visibility
Trees on commercial corridors and high foot-traffic blocks score higher. Visible, well-cared-for trees reflect on the program and are more likely to receive community attention.

Simple 1–6 tier ranking: Young/ESCR status × visibility level.

How Each Tree Is Ranked

Every tree gets a tier from 1 (lowest) to 6 (highest), based on two yes/no questions:

🌱 Is it young or ESCR-planted?
Yes if trunk diameter (DBH) is under 6", or if it appears in LES Ecology Center's ESCR planting records.

👁 How visible is it?
Full visibility: on a named commercial corridor (Delancey, Houston, Bowery, Orchard, Grand, Essex, Rivington, Avenue A, Allen, Forsyth, etc.).
Partial: in a high foot-traffic neighborhood (East Village, Lower East Side, Chinatown) but not on a named corridor.
None: all others.

The 6 tiers
TierYoung/ESCRVisibility
6✅ YesCommercial corridor
5✅ YesHigh-traffic neighborhood
4✅ YesResidential
3⬜ NoCommercial corridor
2⬜ NoHigh-traffic neighborhood
1⬜ NoResidential

How Blocks Are Selected

Census tree data is grouped by NYC block face ID — the official street segment identifier used in the 2015 Street Tree Census. ESCR plantings are matched to the nearest census block within 150 meters.

Only blocks with 5 or more trees are considered. Park and playground trees are excluded — the focus is on street-side trees where volunteer stewardship has the most impact.

Block Ranking Score

Blocks are ranked by a blended score: (number of high-priority trees) × (average priority score of those trees). A tree is considered "high priority" if its score is 45 or above. This rewards blocks that have both many trees needing care and trees with high urgency.

Data Sources

• 2015 NYC Street Tree Census via NYC Open Data (most recent citywide census)

• LES Ecology Center ESCR Planting Records (2019–2024) for newly planted trees

• Addresses geocoded via NYC Planning Geosearch API

Note: Census data is from 2015. Actual tree conditions may differ. Use this list as a starting point for planning stewardship walks.
